beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I finally see why first-order perturbation theory predicts 247 percent: it keeps draining a ground state it assumes stays full, so the probability climbs as a parabola instead of the Rabi sine that turns over at the pi-pulse. I want to pin the crossover. Please: (1) derive the exact two-level Rabi solution on resonance and show it reduces to the parabola when Omega*t is small; (2) find where the two curves visibly diverge as Omega*t approaches pi; (3) state the validity window in terms of Omega*t for trusting perturbation theory.

props.command: I finally see why first-order perturbation theory predicts 247 percent: it keeps draining a ground state it assumes stays full, so the probability climbs as a parabola instead of the Rabi sine that turns over at the pi-pulse. beats.narration_text: Drive an atom with a laser tuned exactly to its resonance. Ask: what is the probability of finding it excited?
Textbook perturbation theory gives a clean formula. Plug in real numbers and it predicts 247 percent — a probability above one.
An impossible answer from a standard formula. What broke — and what actually happens to the atom?

props.headline: An impossible answer from a standard formula. What broke — and…

card.headline:
247% is impossible.
So what does the atom do?
card.dek: Rabi oscillations vs. first-order perturbation theory

beats.narration_text: First-order perturbation theory assumes the atom never leaves the ground state. It keeps draining the ground state as if it were full — even after it has emptied.
So the formula integrates indefinitely, producing a probability that climbs as a parabola — past one, past two, with no ceiling.
The exact two-level solution — Rabi's formula — accounts for the ground state emptying. The probability becomes a sine: it climbs to one, then the atom flips back.
Parabola and sine agree when the product of coupling times time is small — early, weak-drive limit. They diverge as that product approaches pi.
Exactly at the first pi-pulse — where the atom fully flips — perturbation theory predicts 247 percent. The exact formula gives exactly 100 percent.
Rabi oscillations run every qubit, every NMR pulse, every trapped-ion gate. Perturbation theory is not wrong — it is valid only while Omega times t stays much less than one.

Illustrative: two-level atom, h-bar Omega equals 0.01 eV, h-bar omega-zero equals 2 eV. At the pi-pulse, perturbation theory predicts 2.47; Rabi gives exactly 1. Validity window: Omega t well under 0.3.
One parabola, one sine, and a ceiling at probability one. The atom was always bounded; the approximation was not.

props.headline: One parabola, one sine, and a ceiling at probability one. The…

card.headline: P = sin²(Ωt/2)
card.dek: Rabi's formula is bounded. Perturbation theory predicts 247% at the π-pulse — exact answer: 100%.

Perturbation theory is not wrong — it is valid only while Omega times t stays much less than one.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
